title: Project Management
text:
Why Project Management?
Good project management is not just about staying organized — it is a core part of reproducible research. When you structure your project consistently from the start, you make it easier for collaborators, reviewers, and your future self to understand, rerun, and build on your work.
In this workshop series, we embed reproducibility skills directly into foundational coding instruction. This section introduces two practices you will use in every project going forward: initializing a reproducible R environment and writing documentation.

title: Project Management
text:
Creating an R Environment
We use renv to manage package versions in your project. This ensures that anyone who clones your repository can restore the exact same R environment you used — no more “it works on my machine” problems.

Navigate to the Console tab (left side of the panel)
Enter getwd() to check that you are in the directory you cloned. If not, navigate there before continuing.

getwd()

Once you are in the correct directory, enter

renv::init()






NoteWhat renv::init() does




Creates an renv/ folder
Creates an renv.lock file
Creates or updates .gitignore to exclude the renv package cache
Snapshots any packages already detected in the project




Restart r







TipIn the console



You can do this with the circular arrow icon in the upper right of the console window.



To capture your current environment, enter:

renv::snapshot()
This records the package versions currently in use.






TipIn the console



If you get a message that says you are “in a project that hasn’t been activated yet”, choose option 1: Activate the project and use the project library



Take a look at your project directory in the Explorer pane in Positron. In your project directory, you will see new files created by renv:

├── .Rprofile
├── renv.lock
└── renv/
    └── .gitignore    # renv's gitignore, scoped only to the renv/ folder
    └── activate.R
    └── settings.json
    └── library/
Now when this repository is cloned by a collaborator or reviewer, renv::restore() can find the renv files you have created and automatically load the matching package versions.

title: Project Management
text:
Write a README file
A README is the entry point to your project. It tells anyone who opens your repository — including your future self — what the project is, what the data is, and how to use it.

Confirm that you are in the root directory of your workshop folder:

getwd()

In the console, enter:

fs::dir_tree()
This calls the dir_tree function from the fs package - you should see an output that shows you all your project files and their relationships

To write this output to a file, enter this command in the console:

capture.output(fs::dir_tree(), file = "README.txt")

Copy the text in the lines below (stop when you get to step 5) and paste at the top of your README.txt file (open in another window):

This readme file was generated on [YYYY-MM-DD] by [NAME]

GENERAL INFORMATION

Title of Dataset:

Author/Principal Investigator Information

Name: ORCID: Institution: Address: Email:

Date of data collection: provide single date, range, or approximate date; suggested format YYYY-MM-DD)

Geographic location of data collection: provide latitude, longitude, or city/region, State, Country

SHARING/ACCESS INFORMATION

DATA & FILE OVERVIEW

File List:

This README template is a simplified version of Cornell's Readme Template for Data: 
https://ecommons.cornell.edu/entities/publication/6effe964-798e-4c21-aac2-4194709ff230 

Optional: in your README.txt file, write a short description next to these folders:


raw_data (original data goes here)
processed data (clean data goes here)
scripts (code to for analyses goes here)
outputs (analyses outputs go here)

title: Project Management
text:
Scripts for Project Management
The following scripts are used in this workshop series. They are designed to be run in your local Positron session — copy them into your scripts/ folder and run them from there.






Important



Before Running These Scripts
These scripts load data files from your local raw_data/ folder. Make sure you have completed Exercises 1 and 2 and that your folder structure is in place before running them.



Metadata Cleaning for Analysis
This script creates the metadata file needed for further analysis, using the same dataset contrasted with Excel earlier in this workshop.
# Metadata Cleaning for Analysis
# This script creates the metadata file that we need for further analysis

library(dplyr)
library(stringr)

# Load data
metadata <- read.csv("raw_data/GSE113754_filtered_metadata.csv",
                     header = TRUE,
                     stringsAsFactors = TRUE)

# Create the desired output format
metadata_parsed <- metadata %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    # Clean up genotype column - convert "Shank3 Mutant" to "Shank3"
    genotype = case_when(
      str_detect(genotype, "WT") ~ "WT",
      str_detect(genotype, "Shank3") ~ "Shank3",
      TRUE ~ as.character(genotype)
    ),

    # Extract condition from characteristics column,
    # format and store in a new column called "Condition"
    Condition = case_when(
      str_detect(characteristics, "homecage control") ~ "_normal",
      str_detect(characteristics, "sleep deprivation") ~ "_sleep_deprived",
      TRUE ~ "Unknown"
    ),

    # Create Geno_Cond column by combining results from the
    # "genotype" and "Condition" column mutations
    Geno_Cond = paste(genotype, str_remove(Condition, "^_"), sep = "_")
  ) %>%
  # Select only the columns you want in the final output
  dplyr::select(Sample = 1, genotype, Condition, Geno_Cond)

# Check the result
head(metadata_parsed)

# Save to CSV
write.csv(metadata_parsed, "Shank3_metadata_parsed.csv", row.names = FALSE)


Automate File Naming
This script demonstrates how to automatically add a timestamp to any R function and store the result in your outputs/ folder as a new file each time you call it. This ensures you never accidentally overwrite a previous result.
# Automate File Naming
# Automatically timestamps output files and stores them in the outputs/ folder

library(readxl)
library(writexl)
library(dplyr)
library(lubridate)

# Configuration section — update these paths for your project
CONFIG <- list(
  input_file = "raw_data/GSE113754_GeneLevel_Raw_data.xlsx",
  output_folder = "outputs",
  prefix = "LD_ProjectARCS"
)

# Function to generate timestamped filename
generate_filename <- function(prefix, analysis_type = "", extension = ".xlsx") {
  timestamp <- format(Sys.time(), "%Y%m%dT%H%M%S")  # ISO 8601 format
  if (analysis_type != "") {
    filename <- paste0(timestamp, "_", prefix, "_", analysis_type, extension)
  } else {
    filename <- paste0(timestamp, "_", prefix, extension)
  }
  return(filename)
}

# Function to ensure output directory exists
ensure_output_dir <- function(output_folder) {
  if (!dir.exists(output_folder)) {
    dir.create(output_folder, recursive = TRUE)
    cat("Created output directory:", output_folder, "\n")
  }
}

# Function to load Excel data
load_excel_data <- function(file_path, sheet = 1) {
  cat("Loading data from:", file_path, "\n")
  data <- read_excel(file_path, sheet = sheet)
  cat("Data loaded successfully. Dimensions:", dim(data), "\n")
  return(data)
}

# Function to save results
save_results <- function(data, output_folder, prefix, analysis_type = "") {
  ensure_output_dir(output_folder)
  filename <- generate_filename(prefix, analysis_type)
  full_path <- file.path(output_folder, filename)
  write_xlsx(data, full_path)
  cat("Results saved to:", full_path, "\n")
  return(full_path)
}

# Analysis 1: Basic summary statistics
analysis_summary_stats <- function(df, output_folder, prefix) {
  cat("\n=== Running Summary Statistics Analysis ===\n")
  summary_data <- df %>%
    summarise_if(is.numeric, list(
      mean = ~mean(., na.rm = TRUE),
      median = ~median(., na.rm = TRUE),
      sd = ~sd(., na.rm = TRUE),
      min = ~min(., na.rm = TRUE),
      max = ~max(., na.rm = TRUE)
    ))
  save_path <- save_results(summary_data, output_folder, prefix, "summary_stats")
  return(summary_data)
}

# Analysis 2: Filtered data analysis
analysis_filtered_data <- function(df, output_folder, prefix,
                                   filter_condition = NULL) {
  cat("\n=== Running Filtered Data Analysis ===\n")
  if (!is.null(filter_condition)) {
    processed_data <- df %>%
      filter(eval(parse(text = filter_condition))) %>%
      arrange(desc(if(is.numeric(.[,1])) .[,1] else row_number()))
  } else {
    processed_data <- df %>%
      arrange(if(is.numeric(.[,1])) desc(.[,1]) else .[,1])
  }
  save_path <- save_results(processed_data, output_folder, prefix,
                            "filtered_data")
  return(processed_data)
}

# Analysis 3: Grouped analysis
analysis_grouped <- function(df, output_folder, prefix,
                             group_by_col = NULL) {
  cat("\n=== Running Grouped Analysis ===\n")
  if (!is.null(group_by_col) && group_by_col %in% names(df)) {
    grouped_data <- df %>%
      group_by(across(all_of(group_by_col))) %>%
      summarise_if(is.numeric, list(
        count = ~n(),
        mean = ~mean(., na.rm = TRUE),
        sum = ~sum(., na.rm = TRUE)
      ), .groups = 'drop')
  } else {
    first_char_col <- names(select_if(df, is.character))[1]
    if (!is.null(first_char_col)) {
      grouped_data <- df %>%
        count(across(all_of(first_char_col)), name = "frequency") %>%
        arrange(desc(frequency))
    } else {
      grouped_data <- data.frame(note = "No grouping column available")
    }
  }
  save_path <- save_results(grouped_data, output_folder, prefix,
                            "grouped_analysis")
  return(grouped_data)
}

# Main execution function
run_analysis <- function() {
  cat("=== Starting Analysis Pipeline ===\n")
  cat("Timestamp:", format(Sys.time(), "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"), "\n\n")

  original_data <- load_excel_data(CONFIG$input_file)
  working_data <- original_data

  results <- list()
  results$summary <- analysis_summary_stats(
    working_data, CONFIG$output_folder, CONFIG$prefix)
  results$filtered <- analysis_filtered_data(
    working_data, CONFIG$output_folder, CONFIG$prefix)
  results$grouped <- analysis_grouped(
    working_data, CONFIG$output_folder, CONFIG$prefix)

  cat("\n=== Analysis Pipeline Complete ===\n")
  cat("All results saved to:", CONFIG$output_folder, "\n")
  return(results)
}

# Helper function to run individual analyses
run_single_analysis <- function(analysis_type, ...) {
  original_data <- load_excel_data(CONFIG$input_file)
  working_data <- original_data
  switch(analysis_type,
    "summary"  = analysis_summary_stats(working_data,
                   CONFIG$output_folder, CONFIG$prefix),
    "filtered" = analysis_filtered_data(working_data,
                   CONFIG$output_folder, CONFIG$prefix, ...),
    "grouped"  = analysis_grouped(working_data,
                   CONFIG$output_folder, CONFIG$prefix, ...),
    stop("Unknown analysis type. Use 'summary', 'filtered', or 'grouped'")
  )
}

# To use this script:
# 1. Update the CONFIG section above with your file paths
# 2. Run all analyses:
results <- run_analysis()

# Or run individual analyses:
summary_result <- run_single_analysis("summary")
# filtered_result <- run_single_analysis("filtered",
#                      filter_condition = "column_name > 50")
grouped_result <- run_single_analysis("grouped",
                    group_by_col = "category")






Note

✓ Initialize an R environment with renv::init()?
✓ Explain what renv.lock is and why it matters for reproducibility?
✓ Use fs::dir_tree() to inspect your project structure?
✓ Write and populate a README file for a project?

title: Fundamentals of R
text:
Setting Up the Working Directory
What is your working directory? Run getwd() to find out.






TipLearning About Any Function



To learn more about any function, run ?function_name in the console. For example: ?getwd


?getwd()
For the sake of simplifying reading data into R, we need to set our working directory to the folder where our data is located. We will be working from a folder called ARCS_project — this is where your data files and script files live for this workshop series.
In Positron you can set your working directory using the Files pane:

Navigate to your ARCS_project folder
Click the gear icon in the Files pane toolbar
Select “Set as Working Directory”

Notice in your console that a line of code will appear similar to this:
# Mac example
setwd("~/Desktop/ARCS_project")

# Windows example
setwd("C:/Users/YourName/Desktop/ARCS_project")
setwd() is the programmatic way to establish a working directory — you just need to write the full path to your ARCS_project folder on your machine.






TipVerify Your Working Directory



After setting it, confirm you’re in the right place:
getwd()
You should see the path ending in ARCS_project.

Objects simplify data manipulation. Objects result from assigning to a name values or data we want to transform/analyse. This data can then be easily used and re-used. This way, we don’t have to repeat operations.
To create an object, we need:

Tricks and Rules for Creating R Objects

It is good practice to always use <- instead of =







TipKeyboard Shortcut for <-



PC: Alt + -
Mac: Option + - 



An R object can have almost any name. However, there are some important considerations:







ImportantNaming Rules



✗ No empty spaces — the code will not run
✗ Cannot start with numbers (2x is not valid, but x2 is)
✗ Cannot have the same name as functions — it gets confusing

title: Fundamentals of R
text:
Vectors
A vector is the most common and basic data structure in R. A vector is composed by a series of values of one specific data type.
There are 5 main types of data:



Type
Description
Example




double / numeric
Numbers with decimals
1.2


integer
Whole numbers
10L


complex
Numbers with imaginary component
2i


logical
True or False
TRUE


character
String of characters
"Sofia"



To create a vector we use the function c().






NoteFunctions, Arguments, and Packages



Functions like c() are “canned scripts” that automate more complicated sets of commands including operations and assignments.

A function often (but not always) takes one or more inputs called arguments
A function often (but not always) returns a value
Many functions are predefined, or can be made available by importing R packages (more on that later)

To learn more enter ?c() in your computer’s terminal.

Tabular Data
Matrices and data frames are both two-dimensional data structures used to store tabular data (rows and columns), but they differ primarily in the types of data they can contain and their underlying structure.









Data Frame
Matrix




Data types
Can mix types (heterogeneous)
Single type only (homogeneous)


Common use
Data processing, statistics, plotting
Mathematical operations


Structure
List of equal-length vectors
Vector with dimension attributes



Data frames are what we will most commonly use for data processing, statistics, and plotting of tabular data in R.

title: Git & Version Control
text:
Introduction
In this section, we will get hands-on with git right inside Positron. We’ll learn the fundamental workflow of saving changes to our project’s history. This entire process happens locally, on our own computers. We will explore this workflow in two ways: using Positron’s graphical interface and using the command line terminal.
What we’ll cover:

Local git workflow — Making and saving changes to your project history
Best practices — Managing data files with .gitignore to keep repositories lean
Connecting to GitHub — Publishing your local repository to GitHub.com as a remote backup and optionally sharing it publicly
Collaboration — If time permits, we’ll explore how to collaborate with colleagues using Pull Requests (also called Merge Requests), which allow you to propose changes to each other’s projects safely and professionally

title: Git & Version Control
text:
1. Recap: The Initialized Repository
When your Positron Project was created with the option to initialize a git repository, git began tracking your project folder immediately.
Your mission control for git in Positron is the Source Control tab, located in the left activity bar (the same panel used in VS Code).
Action: Click on the Source Control tab. If there are no pending changes, the panel will be empty — which means git doesn’t see any unsaved modifications.

Understanding git Status Indicators
When you make changes to files in your project, they appear in the Source Control panel with status indicators. There are two zones:

Staged Changes (What Will Be Committed)



Indicator
Meaning
What It Tells You




🟢 A
Added
New file staged for commit


🔵 M
Modified
Changed file staged for commit


🔴 D
Deleted
File staged for deletion





Changes (Working Directory — Not Yet Staged)



Indicator
Meaning
What It Tells You




🟡 M
Modified
File has unstaged changes


🟡 U
Untracked
New file git doesn’t know about


🔴 D
Deleted
File deleted but not staged





Common Combinations



Staged
Unstaged
What This Means




(empty)
U
Brand new file, not tracked yet


(empty)
M
Existing file changed, not staged


A
(empty)
New file staged, ready to commit


M
(empty)
Modified file staged, ready to commit


M
M
Some changes staged, more changes unstaged









TipQuick Reference




Staged Changes = What’s packed and ready for your commit
Changes = What’s changed but not staged yet
Clicking the + icon next to a file moves it from Changes → Staged Changes

title: Git & Version Control
text:
2. The git Workflow in Positron
The core git workflow involves three steps:

Make changes to your files
Stage the specific changes you want to save
Commit those staged changes, creating a permanent snapshot in your project’s history


Confirm You Have a git Repository

Check the Source Control tab. Look at the left activity bar in Positron. If you see a Source Control icon with a badge, your repository is initialized. If not, ask your instructor for help.
Open the Terminal. Click on the Terminal tab at the bottom of Positron.
Verify the repository. In the terminal, type:

git status
You should see a message listing “Untracked files” — these are the R scripts and data files you’ve been working with throughout the workshop. This confirms git is watching your project folder but hasn’t saved any snapshots yet.

Check what files you have:

ls
You should see your workshop files like ARCS_Part1.R, ARCS_Part2.R, folders like raw_data/, scripts/, etc.


Your First Commit: Saving Existing Work

Check the Source Control tab. You should see several files listed as untracked (U), indicating git doesn’t know about them yet. You might see files like:

ARCS_Part1.R and ARCS_Part1b.R
ARCS_Part2.R and ARCS_Part2b.R
Shank3_metadata_parsed.csv
Various files in your raw_data/ folder

Stage all files. Open the Terminal tab and type:

git add .
The . means “add all changed files in the current directory and subdirectories.”






NoteAbout Line Ending Warnings



You may see warnings like “CRLF will be replaced by LF the next time Git touches it.” This is normal — git is automatically converting Windows-style line endings to Unix-style for consistency across operating systems. You can safely ignore these warnings.



Check the Source Control tab again. All your files should now appear under Staged Changes, meaning they’re ready to commit.
Commit your work. In the Source Control panel, type your commit message in the message box at the top:
Add workshop scripts and data from Parts 1 and 2
Then click the ✓ Commit button.
View the history. In the terminal, run:

git log --oneline
You can see your first commit listed!
Congratulations — you’ve created your first snapshot of the project!


Creating New Files for Practice

Create a new file. In Positron, go to File → New File → R Script and save it as ARCS_Part3b.R in your scripts folder.
Add a comment:

# This is a placeholder for our script content

View the change. Go back to the Source Control tab. You’ll see ARCS_Part3b.R listed as untracked (U).
Stage the file. Click the + icon next to ARCS_Part3b.R. It will move to Staged Changes with an A indicator.
Commit the change. Type the message Add initial analysis script in the commit message box and click ✓ Commit.

You’ve completed your second version control cycle!

title: Git & Version Control
text:
3. Excluding Data Files: The .gitignore File
Before we continue, let’s discuss an important best practice: not storing large data files in git repositories.

Why Exclude Data Files?
Git is designed to track code and text files, not large data files:

Repository bloat: Data files can make your repository huge and slow to clone, push, and pull
Version control inefficiency: Git tracks every version of every file — a 100MB dataset becomes 500MB after 5 versions
GitHub limitations: GitHub has a 100MB per-file limit and recommends keeping repositories under 1GB
Not designed for data: Git compresses text efficiently but struggles with binary data formats







NoteWhat About Data Storage?



For data files, consider these alternatives:







Data Size
Recommended Approach




Small reference data (< 10MB)
Can include in repository if rarely changes


Medium data (10MB–1GB)
Cloud storage (Dropbox, Google Drive, OneDrive) — document the location


Large data (> 1GB)
Institutional repositories, OSF, Zenodo, or Figshare


Very large data
Git LFS (Large File Storage) — stores large files externally while keeping references in git







Creating a .gitignore File

Create the file. In Positron, go to File → New File → Text File
Add these lines:

# Ignore data folders
raw_data/
processed_data/

# Ignore large output files
outputs/*.pdf
outputs/*.html

# Keep the folder structure but ignore contents
!raw_data/.gitkeep
!processed_data/.gitkeep
!outputs/.gitkeep

Save the file as .gitignore (with the dot!) in your project root folder
Create placeholder files so the folders still exist in git. In the terminal:

touch raw_data/.gitkeep
touch processed_data/.gitkeep
touch outputs/.gitkeep
The .gitkeep files are empty placeholders that preserve your folder structure in the repository even though the data files themselves are ignored.

Commit the .gitignore file. Stage .gitignore and the three .gitkeep files and commit with the message: Add .gitignore to exclude data files







TipVerify What’s Being Tracked



After adding .gitignore, run this to confirm your data files are ignored:
git status --ignored
You should see your data folders listed under “Ignored files”.








ImportantKey Takeaway



Keep your git repository lean by tracking code, not data. Document where your data lives in your README file instead.

title: Git & Version Control
section: 4. git on the Command Line — A Quick Look
text:
4. git on the Command Line — A Quick Look
The Positron Source Control panel is a visual interface that runs git commands for you in the background. Let’s pull back the curtain and run those same commands ourselves.

Open the Terminal in Positron (bottom panel).
Check the project status:

git status
It should say nothing to commit, working tree clean since we just committed all our changes.

Make another change. Go back to ARCS_Part3b.R and add a new line:

# This is our first tracked change.
print("Hello from R!")

Check the status again:

git status
git now sees that ARCS_Part3b.R has been modified.

Stage the change (CLI):

git add .
Run git status once more — you’ll see the file listed under “Changes to be committed.”

Commit the change (CLI):

git commit -m "Add print statement via CLI"
You’ve just done the exact same workflow as before, but entirely from the command line!






NoteKey Takeaway



The Positron Source Control panel is a user-friendly interface for running these fundamental git commands. You can use whichever you prefer, but knowing what’s happening behind the scenes makes you a more confident and capable user.

title: Git & Version Control
section: 5. Practice: Making Commits with GUI and CLI
text:
5. Practice: Making Commits with GUI and CLI

Commit 1: Load and Explore Data (GUI Method)

Add data loading code. Open ARCS_Part3b.R and add these lines:

# ==============================================================================
# ARCS Workshop Part 3 - Gene Expression Analysis
# This script demonstrates data loading and visualization with git version control
# ==============================================================================

library(tidyverse)

# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 1. LOAD DATA
# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------

gene_data <- read_csv("processed_data/clean_data_label.csv")
metadata  <- read_csv("processed_data/clean_meta_data.csv")

# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 2. EXPLORE DATA STRUCTURE
# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------

cat("Dataset dimensions:", nrow(gene_data), "genes x",
    (ncol(gene_data) - 1), "samples\n")

gene_data %>%
  select(1:6) %>%
  head()

cat("\nMetadata structure:\n")
glimpse(metadata)

# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 3. DATA ANALYSIS AND VISUALIZATION
# (Additional analysis code will be added here)
# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Check the Source Control tab. You’ll see ARCS_Part3b.R with a M (modified) indicator.
Review your changes (optional but recommended). Click on the filename in the Source Control panel to open a diff view showing your new code highlighted in green. This is useful for catching mistakes before they become part of your history.
Stage and commit. Click + to stage the file, type the message Add data loading and exploration, and click ✓ Commit.







TipAlways Review the Diff Before Committing



Green highlighting shows additions and red shows deletions. Reviewing your diff before committing helps you catch mistakes, debug code, and avoid committing unwanted changes.




Commit 2: Add Visualization (CLI Method)

Add plotting code. Replace the placeholder comment in Section 3 of ARCS_Part3b.R with:

# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 3. DATA ANALYSIS AND VISUALIZATION
# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------

gnai3_data <- gene_data %>%
  filter(str_detect(names(gene_data)[1], "Gnai3") |
         str_detect(.[[1]], "Gnai3"))

gnai3_long <- gnai3_data %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols      = -1,
    names_to  = "sample_id",
    values_to = "expression"
  ) %>%
  left_join(metadata, by = "sample_id")

gnai3_plot <- ggplot(gnai3_long,
                     aes(x = geno_cond, y = expression, fill = genotype)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7, outlier.shape = NA) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.2, size = 2, alpha = 0.6) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("WT" = "lightblue", "Shank3" = "salmon")) +
  labs(
    title = "Gnai3 Gene Expression by Genotype and Condition",
    x     = "Experimental Group",
    y     = "Read Counts",
    fill  = "Genotype"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title   = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold"),
    axis.text.x  = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)
  )

print(gnai3_plot)

gnai3_summary <- gnai3_long %>%
  group_by(geno_cond, genotype) %>%
  summarise(
    mean = mean(expression),
    sd   = sd(expression),
    n    = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

cat("\nGnai3 Expression Summary Statistics:\n")
print(gnai3_summary)

# ==============================================================================
# END OF SCRIPT
# ==============================================================================

Use the Terminal workflow:

git status
git add scripts/ARCS_Part3b.R
git status
git commit -m "Add Gnai3 expression visualization and summary statistics"

Verify your work:

git log --oneline
You should see all your commits listed.






NoteGUI vs. CLI — When to Use Each










Approach
Best For




GUI (Source Control panel)
Visual learners, reviewing diffs, getting started


CLI (Terminal)
Speed, advanced operations, working on remote servers



Many experienced users mix both: GUI for reviewing diffs, CLI for quick commits.

title: Git & Version Control
text:
6. Local-First vs. Remote-First Repositories

Local-First (What We Just Did)
You start a project on your own computer, initialize a git repository, work on your files, and build a commit history — all locally. Later, you can push this project to GitHub to back it up, share it, or collaborate.
Analogy: You write a document in your text editor on your desktop. When you’re ready, you upload it to cloud storage to share with colleagues.


Remote-First (Cloning)
The project already exists on a remote server like GitHub. Your first step is to clone it — downloading a complete copy of the project and its entire version history to your local computer.
Analogy: A colleague shares a document with you via cloud storage. You download a local copy so you can start editing.






Note



In this workshop we started local-first. In the collaboration section below, you will experience the remote-first workflow when you clone a partner’s repository.

title: Git & Version Control
text:
7. Publishing Your Repository to GitHub
Now that we have a local repository with a commit history, let’s back it up and share it by publishing to GitHub.com. This requires two steps: setting up SSH authentication and creating a remote repository.

Step 1: Set Up SSH Authentication
SSH (Secure Shell) is a secure way to connect your computer to GitHub. Think of it as a special key that proves your identity without typing your password every time.






NoteAlternative: Personal Access Tokens (PATs)



PATs are an alternative authentication method using HTTPS. However, they expire every 90 days and require renewal, which can disrupt your workflow. SSH is strongly preferred for long-term research projects because it is a one-time setup.
If SSH doesn’t work due to network or firewall restrictions, GitHub Desktop (which uses HTTPS) is a good backup option.



Check for Existing SSH Keys
In the Positron Terminal, run:
ls ~/.ssh
If you see files named id_ed25519 or id_rsa, you already have keys — skip to Add Your Key to GitHub below. If not, continue.


Generate a New SSH Key

macOS / LinuxWindows



Generate the key (replace with your GitHub email):

ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "your_email@example.com"

Save the key. Press Enter to accept the default location.
Set a passphrase (optional). Press Enter twice to skip, or type a passphrase you’ll remember.
Start the SSH agent:

eval "$(ssh-agent -s)"

Add your key:

ssh-add ~/.ssh/id_ed25519

Copy your public key:

pbcopy < ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub








NoteWindows Users: Use the Positron Terminal



These commands work in the Positron Terminal or git Bash (installed with git for Windows). We recommend using the Positron Terminal for consistency.



Generate the key (replace with your GitHub email):

ssh-keygen -t rsa -b 4096 -C "your_email@example.com"

Save the key. Press Enter to accept the default location.
Set a passphrase (optional). Press Enter twice to skip.
Start the SSH agent:

eval "$(ssh-agent -s)"

Add your key:

ssh-add ~/.ssh/id_rsa

Copy your public key:

cat ~/.ssh/id_rsa.pub | clip
If clip doesn’t work, run cat ~/.ssh/id_rsa.pub and manually copy the output.






WarningWindows Troubleshooting



If ssh-agent won’t start, try running Positron or git Bash as Administrator. If you see “Could not open a connection to your authentication agent,” run ssh-agent bash first, then retry ssh-add.











ImportantKeep Your Keys Safe!




The .pub file is your public key — safe to share with GitHub
The file without .pub is your private key — never share this
If someone asks for your SSH key, they always mean the public one





Add Your SSH Key to GitHub

Go to github.com and log in
Click your profile photo → Settings
In the left sidebar, click SSH and GPG keys
Click New SSH key
Add a descriptive title like My Positron Laptop
Paste your key in the Key field
Click Add SSH key



Test Your Connection
ssh -T git@github.com
Type yes if prompted about authenticity. You should see: Hi username! You've successfully authenticated...



Step 2: Create a Repository on GitHub.com

Go to github.com and log in
Click your profile picture → Your repositories
Click the green New button
Repository name: e.g., ARCS-workshop
Description (optional): Reproducibility workshop project
Visibility: Private (or Public if you want others to see it)
Important: Do NOT check “Add a README file” — we already have files locally
Click Create repository



Step 3: Connect and Push
GitHub will show setup instructions for “push an existing repository from the command line.” Run these one at a time in your Positron Terminal (replace username and yourreponame with yours):
git remote add origin git@github.com:username/yourreponame.git
git branch -M main
git push -u origin main
Refresh your GitHub repository page — you should see all your files and commit history.






TipTroubleshooting: GitHub Desktop as a Backup Method



If you encounter authorization issues, GitHub Desktop is a reliable alternative:

Download and install GitHub Desktop
Sign in with your GitHub account
Click Add → Add Existing Repository and navigate to your project folder
Click Publish repository

This method uses HTTPS authentication instead of SSH.




GitHub Enterprise Options at Yale
If you’re working with sensitive research data, Yale offers two GitHub Enterprise options beyond the standard GitHub.com platform:

GitHub Enterprise Server (On-Premises)
A self-hosted platform running within Yale’s secure infrastructure. Suitable for version-controlling analysis scripts, maintaining reproducible research pipelines, and integrating with Yale’s research computing resources — all with strict data security and compliance.

Hosted on Yale’s own infrastructure
Data Classification Level Supported: High (suitable for sensitive research data including HIPAA-covered data)
Full control over data location and security policies



GitHub Enterprise Cloud
Adds advanced functionality to GitHub.com, including SAML single sign-on with Yale credentials, additional GitHub Actions minutes, and enhanced security and compliance features.

Cloud-based (managed by GitHub)
Seamless integration with Yale’s authentication systems
Data Classification Level Supported: Moderate (not suitable for high-sensitivity data)







TipChoosing the Right GitHub Platform










Platform
Best For




GitHub.com
Public research, open-source projects, teaching


GitHub Enterprise Cloud
Collaborative research with Yale auth, moderate-sensitivity data


GitHub Enterprise Server
High-sensitivity data, HIPAA compliance, institutional governance



Contact Yale ITS for access to GitHub Enterprise options.
GitHub Enterprise Service & Support: Request access via Yale ServiceNow

title: Git & Version Control
section: 8. Bonus: Collaboration with GitHub Desktop & Positron
text:
8. Bonus: Collaboration with GitHub Desktop & Positron
Now let’s explore one of the most powerful aspects of version control: collaboration. This section demonstrates a complete, real-world collaborative workflow where one person proposes a change to another person’s project.

Understanding the Roles

Project Owner: Owns the original repository and has authority to accept or reject changes
Contributor: Wants to suggest improvements, fixes, or additions



The Fork-and-Pull Request Workflow

Fork — Contributor creates their own complete copy of the project
Clone — They download their copy to work on locally
Branch — They create a separate branch for their contribution
Edit — They make changes and commit them
Push — They upload changes to their fork on GitHub
Pull Request — They formally ask the owner to review and merge



Part 1: The Contributor’s Workflow

Step 1: Fork the Repository

Navigate to the project owner’s repository on GitHub.com
Click the Fork button in the top-right corner
GitHub creates a copy of the entire project under your own account







NoteWhat Forking Actually Does



Forking creates an exact copy of the entire project history — all commits, branches, and files — under your account. You can experiment freely without affecting the original project.




Step 2: Clone Your Fork to GitHub Desktop

In your forked repository, click the green <> Code button
Copy the HTTPS URL
Open GitHub Desktop → File → Clone Repository
Paste the URL, choose a save location, and click Clone



Step 3: Create a New Branch

In GitHub Desktop, click the Current Branch dropdown
Click New Branch
Give it a descriptive name (e.g., add-readme-documentation)
Click Create Branch
Click Publish branch







TipBranch Naming Conventions



Good branch names are descriptive, verb-focused, and lowercase with hyphens:

✓ add-ggplot-visualization
✓ fix-data-loading
✗ new-feature
✗ MyBranch





Step 4: Make Your Edits in Positron

In GitHub Desktop, click Open in Positron (or open the .Rproj file directly)
Create a new Markdown file: File → New File → Markdown File
Save it as README.md in the project root folder
Add the following content:

# Introduction to Programming in R: A Workshop Series 💻

Welcome! This repository was created as part of the 3-part
"Introduction to Programming in R" workshop series, part of the
**Advancing Reproducibility with Computational Skills (ARCS)**
initiative at the Yale Medical Library.

---

## Part 1: Fundamentals and Project Management

### In this workshop, you will learn to:
- Navigate the Positron interface and execute basic R commands
- Apply fundamental programming concepts (objects, functions,
  basic operations)
- Create vectors, data frames, and matrices
- Find a dataset in a public repository and read it into Positron
- Implement file organization and naming conventions for
  reproducible research

### Prerequisites:
- A computer with permissions to install software
- [R](https://cran.r-project.org/) and
  [Positron](https://positron.posit.co/) installation

---

## Part 2: Data Structures and Reports

### In this workshop, you will learn to:
- Manipulate vectors, lists, matrices, and data frames
- Implement literate programming principles in data analysis
- Generate dynamic reports using Quarto for reproducible
  documentation

### Prerequisites:
- Completion of Part 1
- [Quarto](https://quarto.org/docs/get-started/) installation

---

## Part 3: Tidyverse Packages and Git

### In this workshop, you will learn to:
- Execute complex data transformations using `dplyr` and related
  packages
- Initialize git repositories and commit project changes
- Collaborate effectively using GitHub for version control

### Prerequisites:
- Completion of Part 2
- [GitHub Account](https://github.com/signup)
- [GitHub Desktop](https://desktop.github.com/)
- Windows users only: [Git Bash](https://git-scm.com/downloads/)






NoteWhy Add a README?



A README is the first thing people see when they visit a repository. It should explain what the project is about, how to get started, what prerequisites are needed, and how the project is organized. Good documentation makes your research more accessible and reproducible.




Step 5: Commit and Push

Switch back to GitHub Desktop
Review your changes by clicking the file names in the left panel
Write a commit message: Add README documentation for workshop series
Click Commit to [your-branch-name]
Click Push origin



Step 6: Open a Pull Request

After pushing, click Create Pull Request in GitHub Desktop
Give your PR a clear, descriptive title
In the description, explain what you changed and why
Click Create pull request

Example PR description:
## Summary
Added comprehensive README documentation to help users understand
the ARCS workshop series structure, objectives, and prerequisites.

## Changes Made
- Created README.md in the project root
- Documented all three workshop parts with learning objectives
- Listed prerequisites and installation links
- Updated RStudio references to Positron throughout

## Why This Improvement
The repository lacked documentation explaining what the series
covers, how the three parts build on each other, and what software
participants need.



Part 2: The Project Owner’s Review Process

Step 7: Review and Manage the Pull Request

Navigate to your original repository on GitHub.com
Click the Pull requests tab
Click the specific PR to review it
In Files changed, review the exact differences:

Green lines = additions
Red lines = deletions
Leave line-by-line comments for discussion

In Conversation, ask questions, request changes, or approve

Review options:

Approve and Merge: Click Merge pull request → Confirm merge
Request Changes: Leave comments asking for modifications first
Close without Merging: If the contribution doesn’t fit the project’s direction







ImportantCode Review Best Practices




Be constructive: Focus on the code, not the person
Be specific: Point to exact lines and suggest improvements
Be appreciative: Thank contributors for their time and effort
Test locally: For important changes, pull the branch and test it yourself before merging





After Merging
For the Project Owner: - The contribution is now part of the main project - The contributor’s branch can be safely deleted
For the Contributor: - Switch back to main and pull the latest changes - Delete your feature branch — it’s no longer needed - Your contribution is now part of the official project history!



Key Benefits of This Workflow

Safety: The original project is never at risk during development
Review: All changes go through a review process before integration
Discussion: Contributors and maintainers can discuss improvements openly
History: Every contribution is documented with clear attribution
Learning: Contributors improve their skills through feedback








NoteCan You…?




✓ Find the Source Control tab in Positron and interpret the status indicators for staged and unstaged files?
✓ Explain the modify → stage → commit workflow in your own words?
✓ Make a commit using the Positron Source Control panel?
✓ Make a commit using git add and git commit in the terminal?
✓ Explain why large data files should not be stored in git repositories?
✓ Create a .gitignore file and verify it is working with git status --ignored?
✓ Explain the difference between a local-first and remote-first (clone) workflow?
✓ Generate an SSH key and add it to your GitHub account?
✓ Push a local repository to GitHub.com?
✓ Choose the right GitHub platform (GitHub.com, Enterprise Cloud, or Enterprise Server) for a given research scenario?
✓ Fork a repository, create a branch, and open a Pull Request?
✓ Review and merge an incoming Pull Request as a project owner?

section: Importing data into R using Rio() and Here()
text: Importing data into R using Rio() and Here()

title: Tidyverse
text:
Quick background

Base R (no packages)

This is the original, old school way of reading .csv, .txt, and .dat files and returns a data frame in the R environment using the function read.csv, read.table, etc.
Cumbersome - it requires additional manual coding specifications based on the file type; it can be slow for large datasets; and can lead to unexpected behaviors with certain column types (i.e. converting character columns into factors)



readr package

Modern alternative to Base R and requires less configuration while being faster, cleaner, and provides less coding headaches (its does not convert character columns into factors) and uses the function read_csv, read_tsv, etc.
Returns a tibble, a modern data frame with features that make it print nicely, displays on a screen better, does not change variables names, and does not use row labels. The output when inspecting the data set using str() are nicer looking and more informative







Noteread_csv() vs read.csv()



This section uses read_csv() from the Tidyverse rather than read.csv() from base R. Key differences:



Feature
read_csv()
read.csv()




Column types
Explicit (col_double, etc.)
Inferred


Speed
Faster on large files
Slower


Output
Tibble
Data frame


Column names
Preserved as-is
May be altered


Row labels
Not used
Supported



A tibble is a modern data frame that prints more cleanly, displays better on screen, and produces more informative output from functions like str().




haven package

The haven package is the dedicated tool for reading proprietary formats like SPSS (.sav), SAS (.xpt & .sas7bdat), and Stata (.dta), and is a key part of the Tidyverse ecosystem.



openxlsx package

The openxlsx package is the dedicated tool for reading and writing data from Excel files (.xlsx) without needing external dependencies like Java. It is widely used for working with multi-sheet workbooks, applying styles, and handling complex Excel features, offering functions like read.xlsx() for import.

title: Tidyverse
section: Why use the rio and here packages instead of base R?
text:
Why use the rio and here packages instead of base R?

rio package

Is highly useful because it provides a single, universal function for importing virtually any data file format.
The import() function automatically detects the file type based on the file extension (e.g., .csv, .xlsx, .RData, .rds, .xpt, .sas7bdat, .dta, .sav, .json,.txt, .dat) and uses the appropriate specialized package (readr,haven, and openxlsx) to import it.



here package

The package here and its function here() make it easy to tell R where to find and to save your files - in essence, it builds file paths.
Using here() works with you R Project to eliminate path problems -> Relative paths = good for sharing; Absolute paths = Bad for sharing

Bad: "/Users/mwegener/Desktop/maybe_here/data.csv"
Good: here("raw_data", "data.csv")

This is useful when the R project may be shared or accessed by multiple people/computers

title: Tidyverse
section: Data cleaning and validation using the Tidyverse
text:
Data cleaning and validation using the Tidyverse
The tidyverse is a collection of R packages designed around shared principles for efficient, readable data analysis. Install all packages at once with install.packages("tidyverse") and load with library(tidyverse).

Core Philosophy:

Tidy data structure: Each variable = column, each observation = row, each value = cell
Consistent syntax: Predictable function behavior across all packages
Human-centered design: Intuitive functions that reduce learning curve
Pipe workflows: Use %>% (|>) to chain operations for readable, step-by-step analysis



Key Benefits:

Uniform data structures using data frames/tibbles
Reproducible analysis with version control emphasis
Clean naming conventions (lowercase_with_underscores)
Active community support and best practices
Functional pipelines that transform data through logical steps

The selection and inspection of specific elements or subsets of data are made easier in R by the use of an element’s numerical position, or index.

Slides

title: Reports
text:
Why Reports?
Quarto documents let you combine code, results, and narrative text in a single file that can be rendered to HTML, PDF, or Word. This means your analysis and its documentation live together — when your code changes, your report updates automatically. This is a core reproducibility practice.

title: Reports
section: Convert README.txt to a Quarto Document
text:
Convert README.txt to a Quarto Document

Navigate to your README.txt file in your ARCS project folder (PC: Windows Explorer; Mac: Finder)
Right click on the file and select Rename
Change the file extension from .txt to .qmd (Mac: type .qmd after README)
Accept the file extension change and save the file
Go back to Positron and open your README.qmd file
Add triple backticks ( ``` ) before and after your README’s file structure tree to format it as a code block
In Positron, click the Render button to render your document
A browser window should open and display your README.qmd file as HTML







NoteWhat is localhost?



Notice that the browser window’s address starts with localhost followed by a port number. This means your computer is serving your .qmd file locally to itself — the file is not on the internet, it is being previewed from your own machine.

title: Reports
text:
Automate README.qmd Updates
Instead of manually updating your README every time your project structure changes, we can write an R code chunk that generates the file tree automatically each time you render.

Check whether you have the fs package installed:

library(fs)
If you get an error, install it first:
install.packages("fs")

Copy the script below:

#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| results: asis

library(fs)

# Get the actual structure
tree_output <- capture.output(dir_tree(".", recurse = 2))

# Define comments for specific files/folders
comments <- list(
  "raw_data"       = " (original data goes here)",
  "processed_data" = " (clean data goes here)",
  "scripts"        = " (code goes here)",
  "outputs"        = " (results and charts go here)",
  "README.qmd"     = " (project documentation)"
)

# Add comments to matching lines
for(item in seq_along(tree_output)) {
  line <- tree_output[item]
  for(name in names(comments)) {
    if(grepl(name, line)) {
      tree_output[item] <- paste0(line, comments[[name]])
      break
    }
  }
}

# Print the result
cat("```\n")
cat(paste(tree_output, collapse = "\n"))
cat("\n```\n")






NoteWhat this script does




dir_tree() generates a visual tree of your project folder up to 2 levels deep
capture.output() stores that tree as a character vector
The comments list defines annotations to add next to specific folders or files
The for loop finds matching lines and appends the annotations
cat() prints the result as a formatted code block in your rendered document




Paste this script into your README.qmd file in between the triple backticks where your file tree currently appears
Next to the opening triple backticks, add {r} to make it an R code chunk — it should look like this: ```{r}
Save your README.qmd file
Click the Render button in Positron
Go back to your browser window to see the result
Make any changes you would like to the comments <- list() section and re-render to see them reflected

title: Reports
section: Create a Quarto Document for Publishing
text:
Create a Quarto Document for Publishing
In this exercise you will create a publication-quality document using a PLOS journal template, run a gene variability analysis, and generate a heatmap figure.

Setup

Check whether you have the following packages installed:

library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)
library(scales)
If any are missing, install them in your console:
install.packages("tidyverse")
install.packages("readxl")
install.packages("scales")






TipChecking installed packages in Positron



You can also check in the Extensions pane by searching for the package name.




Install the PLOS Template

In your Terminal tab, run the following commands one at a time:

quarto add quarto-journals/plos
quarto use template quarto-journals/plos






NoteWhat this does



This installs a Quarto extension that formats your document to match PLOS journal submission requirements — including layout, fonts, and figure formatting. This is the same approach used for many other journal templates available through quarto-journals.




Gene Variability Analysis

Copy the code below and paste it into your publication.qmd file underneath the heading ## Gene Variability Analysis. Start your code block with ```{r} and close it with ```.







NoteAbout the #| lines



These are chunk options — they tell Quarto how to handle the output when this code runs inside a .qmd file. message: false suppresses package loading messages, and warning: false suppresses warnings.


#| message: false
#| warning: false

# Prepare data - rename the first column and use both ID columns
gene_data_long <- gene_data %>%
  rename(ensembl_id = `...1`) %>%
  filter(!is.na(gene_symbol), gene_symbol != "") %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = -c(ensembl_id, gene_symbol),
               names_to = "sample",
               values_to = "expression")

# Calculate variance for each gene across all samples
gene_variability <- gene_data_long %>%
  group_by(gene_symbol, ensembl_id) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_expression        = mean(expression, na.rm = TRUE),
    variance               = var(expression, na.rm = TRUE),
    coefficient_of_variation = sd(expression, na.rm = TRUE) / mean_expression,
    .groups = 'drop'
  )

# Identify top 15 most variable genes
top_genes <- gene_variability %>%
  arrange(desc(variance)) %>%
  slice_head(n = 15)

# Display summary statistics
cat("Total genes analyzed:", nrow(gene_variability), "\n")
cat("Mean variance across all genes:",
    round(mean(gene_variability$variance, na.rm = TRUE), 2), "\n")
cat("Most variable gene:", top_genes$gene_symbol[1],
    "with variance =", round(top_genes$variance[1], 2), "\n")

# Show the top 10 most variable genes
cat("\nTop 10 most variable genes:\n")
print(top_genes %>%
        select(gene_symbol, variance) %>%
        slice_head(n = 10) %>%
        mutate(variance = round(variance, 2)))


Data Visualization

Copy the code below and paste it into your publication.qmd file underneath the heading ## Data Visualization. Start your code block with ```{r} and close it with ```.

#| fig-cap: "Expression heatmap showing scaled expression levels of the
#|   15 most variable genes across all samples. Blue indicates
#|   below-average expression, white indicates average expression,
#|   and red indicates above-average expression for each gene."
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 6

# Prepare heatmap data
heatmap_data <- gene_data_long %>%
  filter(gene_symbol %in% top_genes$gene_symbol) %>%
  left_join(top_genes %>% select(gene_symbol, variance),
            by = "gene_symbol") %>%
  group_by(gene_symbol) %>%
  mutate(scaled_expr = scale(expression)[, 1]) %>%
  ungroup()

# Create heatmap visualization
expression_heatmap <- heatmap_data %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = sample,
             y = reorder(gene_symbol, variance),
             fill = scaled_expr)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 0.1) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low   = "blue",
    mid   = "white",
    high  = "red",
    name  = "Scaled\nExpression",
    limits = c(-3, 3),
    oob   = scales::squish
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Expression Variability Heatmap: Top 15 Most Variable Genes",
    subtitle = "Genes ordered by variance (highest at top)",
    x        = "Sample ID",
    y        = "Gene Symbol"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    axis.text.x  = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, size = 8),
    axis.text.y  = element_text(size = 10, face = "italic"),
    plot.title   = element_text(hjust = 0.5, face = "bold", size = 14),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 12),
    legend.position = "right",
    panel.grid   = element_blank()
  )

print(expression_heatmap)

# Save high-resolution figure
ggsave(paste0("outputs/", timestamp, "_expression_heatmap.png"),
       plot   = expression_heatmap,
       width  = 10,
       height = 6,
       dpi    = 300)

Render your publication.qmd document to see your PLOS-formatted article







TipSaving your figure



Notice the ggsave() call at the end of the visualization block. This saves a high-resolution .png to your outputs/ folder with a timestamp in the filename — the same automated file naming pattern introduced in the Project Management section.

text:
True Story: Data Management Gone Awry


Here’s an example of what can happen when you don’t make data management decisions.
This screenshot is from a publication from a Yale School of Medicine lab. It was published in a Nature journal. You’ll notice the bright outlines in the images above, which were identified by an AI image recognition tool (imagetwin.ai)as being duplicative of each other. A human peer found these duplicative images in 2025 and this discussion I’m reporting comes from a public journal club platform (https://pubpeer.com/publications/1E57F80A4418124605D36D4B734480#)

text:
Data Processing


Excel

Processes one observation at a time
Opportunity for copy/paste errors
Steps are not documented
New file means redoing all steps
Setup time may be shorter


R

Processes entire vectors of observations
No copy/paste errors during processing
Code can document itself
New file means a small change (if any)
Setup may take longer

Practical implications
Excel is looping behind a friendly interface, which means that:
Excel hits walls at:

~50,000 rows (becomes very slow)
~1 million rows (Excel’s row limit)
Complex nested formulas (exponential slowdown)

R scales to:

Millions of rows routinely
Billions of rows with proper techniques
Complex operations remain fast




The big picture: Compared to Excel, these advantages can be a part of your workflow with R: scalability, self-documentation, automation, reproduciblity.

title:
Introduction to
Programming in R Part 1:
Project Management
text:
What Are R Projects?

Self-contained workspace for each analysis or research project
Automatic working directory management. No more setwd()
Seamless integration with version control and documentation tools


Think of an R Project like having a dedicated laboratory bench for each experiment. Just like you wouldn’t mix chemicals from different experiments on the same bench, you shouldn’t mix files from different analyses in the same folder. When you open an R Project, R automatically “knows” where all your files are located - your data, scripts, outputs, and documentation all live together. This eliminates one of the most common beginner frustrations: the dreaded “Error: cannot open file” because R can’t find your data. The working directory is automatically set to your project folder, so file paths just work.

title:
Introduction to
Programming in R Part 1:
Project Management
section: WHEN and WHERE to Create R Projects
text:
WHEN and WHERE to Create R Projects

WHEN: At the very beginning, before writing any code
WHERE: Create a dedicated folder for each project inside of one “Code” or “R_Projects” folder
One R project = one research question/paper/analysis (not one project for all your R work)


The biggest mistake beginners make is creating one giant “R_Work” folder and throwing everything in there. Instead, think of each research question or manuscript as deserving its own project. Create the R Project FIRST, before you even download your data. This is like setting up your lab notebook before starting an experiment - it ensures everything stays organized from day one. Choose descriptive names that will make sense to you (and your collaborators) months later. A good rule of thumb: if you might publish this as a separate paper, it should be its own R Project. The folder location should be somewhere logical on your computer - many people create a “Research” or “Projects” folder in their Documents.

title:
Introduction to
Programming in R Part 1:
Project Management
section: Data Management Advantages to R Projects
text:
Data Management Advantages to R Projects

Relative file paths work everywhere

input_file <- "raw_data/GSE113754_GeneLevel_Raw_data.xlsx" #this
input_file <- "C:/Users/Username/Code/ARCS/raw_data/GSE113754_GeneLevel_Raw_data.xlsx" #NOT this

Share projects easily (zip the folder, send to collaborators/your future self)
Clear project boundaries (everything stays together)


Here’s where R Projects really shine for reproducibility. When you use relative paths like “data/metadata.csv”, your code works on any computer - yours, your advisor’s, your collaborator’s - as long as they have the project folder. No more changing file paths when you switch from your laptop to the lab computer! The automatic working directory means you’ll never again see that frustrated student debugging session where half the time is spent figuring out where R is looking for files. When you want to share your analysis, just zip the entire project folder - everything your collaborator needs is in one place. RStudio keeps track of your recent projects, so you can easily switch between your Shank3 analysis and your methods comparison without confusion. This organization becomes crucial when you’re juggling multiple research projects simultaneously.


.Rproj

Opening RStudio from an .Rproj file automatically sets the working directory to that folder
Lets RStudio remember project-specific session settings (like which files were open)
Your project appears in RStudio’s recent projects list
Everything just works - No need to hunt for files or set paths

The .Rproj File: Your Project’s “ID Card”
What it does: The .Rproj file is like an ID card that tells RStudio “this folder is a special R project folder.”
What happens when you double-click it: RStudio opens and automatically sets the working directory to that folder RStudio “remembers” project-specific settings (like which files were open) Your project appears in RStudio’s recent projects list Everything “just works” - no need to hunt for files or set paths
DEMO: if I click on a new .Rproj file, then go to terminal, pwd will show my new directory. If I ls, I will see the files that are in my directory.

title:
Introduction to
Programming in R Part 1:
Project Management
text:
What is Git?

Version control system - like track changes for your entire project, not just one document
Takes snapshots of your project at different points in time
Remembers what changed
Works locally on your computer
Different from GitHub - Git is the technology, GitHub is the website
Every R Project can use Git


The .gitignore file is our first encounter with version control using git. Has anyone heard about this technology or used it before? What do you know about it? This is a technology that you will learn more about through the GitHub platform in part 3 of this class.
For now, you just need to know that git is a more powerful way of doing something like tracking changes and resolving differences if you and perhaps your collaborators iterate through different versions of a script and expand your project, or, if you need to roll back to an earlier version of your project, you can do that with Git. Git creates an audit-proof version of record for the coding work that you do. It can be one very important part of making your work reproducible.
Now, since Git pays attention to EVERY file in your project, we need to tell it which files to ignore - and that’s where .gitignore comes in…

title:
Introduction to
Programming in R Part 1:
Project Management
text:
Building Reproducible Projects


What we just built:

Organized File Structure - dedicated folders
Protected Raw Data - original data stays untouched
R Project + Git - portable, trackable, self-contained
Complete README - project map, instructions, data sources




Why this matters:

Makes your project understandable
Anyone can run your analysis
Anyone can verify your results
Future-proofs your projects (when initialized early and modified regularly)



What we just did in this exercise is build the foundation of reproducible computational research. We created two things that work together: structure and documentation.
The file structure- separating raw data from processed data, organizing scripts and outputs - that’s not just about being neat. It creates a clear audit trail that anyone can follow. Your raw data folder is like evidence that stays untouched, while your folder structure shows exactly how data flows through your analysis.
The README we created is what makes your structured project actually usable. You could have the most organized folders in the world, but without documentation explaining what everything does and how to use it, it’s still a mystery to others - and to your future self.
Together, these create true computational reproducibility. Someone else can look at your project, quickly understand what you did, run your analysis, and get the same results. That’s the gold standard in research - work that others can independently verify.
Six months from now, when you’ve forgotten the details, this combination of clear organization and good documentation will be your lifeline. Ideally, you’ll open your project, read your README, and immediately know exactly what you were doing and how to continue.
This isn’t just good practice - it’s what separates amateur data analysis from trustworthy research, and what protects you as a researcher when funders, publishers, and peers ask you to show your work.

title:
Introduction to
Programming in R Part 1:
Project Management
text:
Filenaming Conventions

No spaces (use underscore or en dash)
Start with dates using International Organization for Standardization (ISO)

YYYYMMDDHHMMSS

Use descriptive names


Let’s talk about something that seems simple but will save you hours of frustration: naming your files well.
First rule: no spaces in file names. Spaces are often special characters in programming languages that have a specific assigned use behind the scenes. Or, they may keep your computer from understanding that words belong together. Use underscores or dashes instead - both work fine, just pick one and stick with it.
For dates, I want you to learn the ISO standard: YYYY-MM-DD. So October 2, 2025 becomes 20251002. Why? Because when your computer sorts files alphabetically, they’ll automatically sort in chronological order too. The American date format won’t do this.
Be descriptive with your names. In six months when you’re looking through your files, ‘data.csv’ tells you nothing, but ‘participant_demographics.csv’ immediately tells you what’s inside.
The key is consistency. Whatever naming pattern you choose, use it for everything in your project. Your future self will thank you when you can find files instantly instead of opening each one to see what’s inside.”
